# Python Enterprise CRUD Demo

This guide introduces a small enterprise application built with Python, Streamlit, and Microsoft SQL Server.

The goal is not to learn every technical detail at once. Start with the small practice repositories, check that your setup works, and then move to the full CRUD application.

:::{.callout-note}
Use this page as orientation. The concrete commands and code examples are explained in the later chapters.
:::

## Learning Path

Follow the chapters in this order:

| Step | What you do | Where |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | Install the required tools | [Windows Setup](windows-setup.qmd) or [macOS Setup](mac-setup.qmd) |
| 2 | Connect VS Code to SQL Server | [MSSQL in VS Code](mssql-vscode.qmd) |
| 3 | Test the small practice repositories | [Practice Repositories](practice-repositories.qmd) |
| 4 | Understand the application's database connection | [Server Connection](mssql-connection.qmd) |
| 5 | Run and inspect the Streamlit app | [Streamlit Application](streamlit.qmd) |

## Repositories

The course uses three GitHub repositories:

| Repository | Purpose |
| --- | --- |
| [mssql-example](https://github.com/kirenz/mssql-example) | Test a basic Python connection to SQL Server |
| [streamlit-mssql](https://github.com/kirenz/streamlit-mssql) | Run a small Streamlit app with SQL Server data |
| [sopra](https://github.com/kirenz/sopra) | Work with the full layered CRUD application |

Keep the repositories in separate folders. Each repository is a separate learning step.

## What This Project Demonstrates

The full application manages quantity-based B2B discounts. It shows how a typical Python application can:

- read from and write to Microsoft SQL Server
- keep database credentials out of the source code
- separate the user interface from business logic and database access
- use database views for controlled enterprise data access

## Architecture At A Glance

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
    U["Browser"] --> A["Streamlit UI"]
    A --> B["Business Logic"]
    B --> C["Database Access"]
    C --> D["SQL Server Views"]
    D --> E["SQL Server"]
```

You do not need to understand every layer before starting. The important idea is that each layer has one clear responsibility.

## What To Focus On First

When working through the material, focus on these questions:

- Can Python connect to the SQL Server database?
- Can Streamlit display data from SQL Server in the browser?
- Where does the full application handle user input, business rules, and SQL access?

If one step fails, fix that step before moving on. This keeps setup problems separate from application design problems.
